title: Counting Representations of Fuchsian Groups Over Finite Fields
speaker: Michael Larsen
abstract: Recent progress on character bounds for groups of Lie type makes it feasible in many cases to find the asymptotic growth, for fixed q and n tending to infinity, of the number of n-dimensional representations of a Fuchsian group G over the field with q elements. Applications include determining the dimensions of complex representation varieties of Fuchsian groups in high degree and proving the existence of strongly Zariski-dense Fuchsian subgroups, in the sense of Breuillard-Green-Guralnick-Tao, of classical groups of high rank.

proof_stages.label: Fuchsian representation-counting setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the cocompact oriented Fuchsian group by generators, torsion relations, and negative orbifold Euler characteristic, and sets up the problem of counting Hom(gamma,G(F_q)) for G=GL_n or SL_n from both algebraic-geometric and finite-character-theoretic viewpoints.

proof_stages.label: Target applications and asymptotic predictions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates two goals: fixed-q, n-to-infinity asymptotics with a periodic modular-form-type multiplier, and strongly dense embeddings of Fuchsian groups into large SL_n, with odd torsion orders as a necessary condition.

proof_stages.label: Surface-group model via Frobenius point counts
proof_stages.summary: For the torsion-free surface-group case, Frobenius's character formula and character-degree estimates for GL_n(F_q) show that degree-one characters dominate the point count, giving the expected irreducible dimension and, via a fiber-dimension argument, irreducibility of the representation variety.

proof_stages.label: Specialization strategy for strong density
proof_stages.summary: Using the initial Zariski-dense SL_2 representation and the irreducibility of the representation variety, many embeddings SL_2 into SL_n specialize from one generic representation, forcing the generic Zariski closure to be all of SL_n.

proof_stages.label: Hurwitz formula with prescribed torsion classes
proof_stages.summary: For Fuchsian groups with torsion, homomorphisms are stratified by the conjugacy classes containing the torsion generators, and Hurwitz's formula expresses each stratum through class sizes and irreducible character values.

proof_stages.label: Character-value estimates for finite classical groups
proof_stages.summary: To make Hurwitz sums asymptotically tractable, the argument requires support-dependent bounds on |chi(g)|, refined from earlier split semisimple estimates to a non-unipotent estimate with exponent 1 minus support/n plus epsilon_q.

proof_stages.label: Torsion asymptotic theorem and theta-series main term
proof_stages.summary: Under q larger than an absolute constant and Euler characteristic less than -1/6, the character estimates yield the predicted fixed-q, n-to-infinity asymptotic, whose periodic factor arises from lattice sums over well-balanced finite-order conjugacy classes and hence from theta functions.

proof_stages.label: Well-balanced components for strongly dense Fuchsian embeddings
proof_stages.summary: The irreducibility of the well-balanced torsion strata suffices for strong density because large symmetric-power blocks of SL_2 representations distribute torsion eigenvalues evenly, putting many specializations in the same component and enabling the same generic-closure argument.

examples.name: Small triangle groups near zero Euler characteristic
examples.note: Triangle groups such as the odd (3,3,5) case and the (2,3,7)/(2,3,n) cases illustrate the obstruction created when the orbifold Euler characteristic is too close to zero.
examples.engagement_reason: small triangle groups such as 2-3-7, 2-3-8, and 2-3-n are cited as near-zero-Euler-characteristic exceptions or problematic cases, but their invariants are not explicitly worked out

examples.name: Order-15 torsion generator mapping to order 5
examples.note: The speaker uses the possibility that a generator of order 15 maps to an element of order 5 to clarify why prescribed conjugacy classes must have orders dividing the torsion orders in Hurwitz's formula.
examples.engagement_reason: the order-15 generator mapping to order 5 is only given as a possible torsion-image scenario before summing over divisor orders, with no computation carried out on it

passes.reason: A C15 torsion generator sent to order 5 is a collapsed/nonfaithful cyclic image, used to show the image order need only divide a_i rather than equal it.
A map from an order-15 torsion generator to order 5 collapses the cyclic subgroup and is used to show exact torsion order need not be preserved.
The order-15 torsion generator is allowed to collapse to a proper divisor-order image, showing exact order need not be preserved.
It illustrates a proper-divisor torsion image, collapsing the order-15 generator and showing exact order need not be preserved.
the image order drops to a proper divisor of the prescribed torsion order, illustrating that exact-order images need not occur
the map collapses an exact order-15 torsion element to a proper-divisor order and is used to show images need not have the same order.
It shows exact order need not be preserved, since the order-15 torsion relation collapses to a proper divisor order in the image.
It is used to show that a torsion generator need not map to an element of the same order, and the cyclic action is collapsed from order 15 to order 5.
It illustrates the naive exact-order requirement failing by collapsing a C15 torsion generator to a C5 image.
used to show a torsion generator need only map to a divisor-order element, with the cyclic order collapsed from 15 to 5
It shows that exact torsion order need not be preserved, with the order-15 cyclic torsion collapsed to an order-5 image.
It illustrates the failure of exact-order preservation under homomorphism, with the cyclic torsion collapsed from order 15 to order 5.

examples.name: Split order-three conjugacy class in GL_n(F_q)
examples.note: Working out the eigenvalue multiplicities for an order-three class with q congruent to 1 mod 3 produces the lattice sum and centralizer quadratic form that explain the theta-series factor.
examples.engagement_reason: the split order-three class in GL_n(F_q) is analyzed by listing eigenvalue multiplicities, translating them to a lattice, identifying balanced classes, computing centralizer structure, and deriving the quadratic form leading to a theta series

passes.reason: A split semisimple order-three conjugacy class in GL_n(F_q) is an ordinary systematic family parametrized by eigenvalue multiplicities, used illustratively for the theta-series calculation.

examples.name: One-relator and hyperbolic groups as a comparison class
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker contrasts Fuchsian groups with one-relator or hyperbolic groups to show that the method depends on both an initial SL_2 representation and a usable character-counting formula.
examples.engagement_reason: one-relator and hyperbolic groups are discussed only as a possible comparison or extension class, with general obstacles noted rather than a concrete example being analyzed
